title: New York State Route 15A

infobox.value: Designated NY 2A in 1930;[[2]](#cite_note-gb-2) renumbered to NY 15A in April 1939[[3]](#cite_note-1939changes-3)

[Livingston](/wiki/Livingston_County,_New_York "Livingston County, New York"), [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario_County,_New_York "Ontario County, New York"), [Monroe](/wiki/Monroe_County,_New_York "Monroe County, New York")

* [Interstate](/wiki/List_of_Interstate_Highways_in_New_York "List of Interstate Highways in New York") * [US](/wiki/List_of_U.S._Routes_in_New_York "List of U.S. Routes in New York") * [State](/wiki/List_of_state_routes_in_New_York "List of state routes in New York") * [Reference](/wiki/List_of_reference_routes_in_New_York "List of reference routes in New York") * [Parkways](/wiki/Parkways_in_New_York "Parkways in New York") | |

(**NY 15A**) is a north–south [state highway](/wiki/State_highway "State highway") located in the [western portion of New York](/wiki/Western_New_York "Western New York") in the United States. It serves as an easterly alternate route of [NY 15](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_15 "New York State Route 15"), beginning in the [Livingston County](/wiki/Livingston_County,_New_York "Livingston County, New York") town of [Springwater](/wiki/Springwater,_New_York "Springwater, New York") and ending 35.19 miles (56.63 km) to the north in the [Monroe County](/wiki/Monroe_County,_New_York "Monroe County, New York") city of [Rochester](/wiki/Rochester,_New_York "Rochester, New York"). NY 15A meets [U.S. Route 20A](/wiki/U.S._Route_20A_(New_York) "U.S. Route 20A (New York)") (US 20A) in the [hamlet](/wiki/Hamlet_(New_York) "Hamlet (New York)") of [Hemlock](/wiki/Hemlock,_New_York "Hemlock, New York"), [US 20](/wiki/U.S._Route_20_in_New_York "U.S. Route 20 in New York") and [NY 5](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_5 "New York State Route 5") in the [village of Lima](/wiki/Lima_(village),_New_York "Lima (village), New York"), and [Interstate 390](/wiki/Interstate_390 "Interstate 390") (I-390) twice in [Henrietta](/wiki/Henrietta,_New_York "Henrietta, New York") and [Brighton](/wiki/Brighton,_Monroe_County,_New_York "Brighton, Monroe County, New York"). Its southern and middle sections traverse mostly rural areas, while the northernmost piece in Henrietta, Brighton, and Rochester passes through significantly more developed areas.

as part of the [1930 renumbering of state highways in New York](/wiki/1930_state_highway_renumbering_(New_York) "1930 state highway renumbering (New York)"). Two parts of the route were sub-standard at the time; however, those sections were rebuilt in the early and mid-1930s. NY 2 was supplanted by an extended [US 15](/wiki/U.S._Route_15_in_New_York "U.S. Route 15 in New York") in April 1939, at which time NY 2A was renumbered to NY 15A to match its parent's new designation.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

State highway in western New York, US

This article is about the current alignment of NY 15A. For the former alignment of NY 15A in Seneca County, see [New York State Route 96A](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_96A "New York State Route 96A").

**New York State Route 15A** (**NY 15A**) is a north–south [state highway](/wiki/State_highway "State highway") located in the [western portion of New York](/wiki/Western_New_York "Western New York") in the United States. It serves as an easterly alternate route of [NY 15](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_15 "New York State Route 15"), beginning in the [Livingston County](/wiki/Livingston_County,_New_York "Livingston County, New York") town of [Springwater](/wiki/Springwater,_New_York "Springwater, New York") and ending 35.19 miles (56.63 km) to the north in the [Monroe County](/wiki/Monroe_County,_New_York "Monroe County, New York") city of [Rochester](/wiki/Rochester,_New_York "Rochester, New York"). NY 15A meets [U.S. Route 20A](/wiki/U.S._Route_20A_(New_York) "U.S. Route 20A (New York)") (US 20A) in the [hamlet](/wiki/Hamlet_(New_York) "Hamlet (New York)") of [Hemlock](/wiki/Hemlock,_New_York "Hemlock, New York"), [US 20](/wiki/U.S._Route_20_in_New_York "U.S. Route 20 in New York") and [NY 5](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_5 "New York State Route 5") in the [village of Lima](/wiki/Lima_(village),_New_York "Lima (village), New York"), and [Interstate 390](/wiki/Interstate_390 "Interstate 390") (I-390) twice in [Henrietta](/wiki/Henrietta,_New_York "Henrietta, New York") and [Brighton](/wiki/Brighton,_Monroe_County,_New_York "Brighton, Monroe County, New York"). Its southern and middle sections traverse mostly rural areas, while the northernmost piece in Henrietta, Brighton, and Rochester passes through significantly more developed areas.

From 1927 to the late 1930s, modern [NY 15](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_15 "New York State Route 15") was part of [NY 2](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_2_(1927%E2%80%931939) "New York State Route 2 (1927–1939)"). As a result, all of what is now NY 15A was originally designated as **New York State Route 2A** as part of the [1930 renumbering of state highways in New York](/wiki/1930_state_highway_renumbering_(New_York) "1930 state highway renumbering (New York)"). Two parts of the route were sub-standard at the time; however, those sections were rebuilt in the early and mid-1930s. NY 2 was supplanted by an extended [US 15](/wiki/U.S._Route_15_in_New_York "U.S. Route 15 in New York") in April 1939, at which time NY 2A was renumbered to NY 15A to match its parent's new designation.

## Route description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_15A&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Route description")]

### Livingston and Ontario Counties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_15A&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Livingston and Ontario Counties")]

NY 15A begins at an intersection with [NY 15](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_15 "New York State Route 15") in the town of [Springwater](/wiki/Springwater,_New_York "Springwater, New York") hamlet of the same name, also officially known as Springwater Hamlet, located on the east side of a large valley in southeastern [Livingston County](/wiki/Livingston_County,_New_York "Livingston County, New York"). The route heads northward as North Main Street, proceeding along the eastern base of the valley as it passes through the northern half of the community. Outside of the [hamlet](/wiki/Hamlet_(New_York) "Hamlet (New York)"), NY 15A enters a rural, undeveloped area dominated by dense forests to the east and a low-lying, slightly more open area to the west. As the route approaches the Livingston–[Ontario](/wiki/Ontario_County,_New_York "Ontario County, New York") county line, the lowlands give way to marshes, which in turn lead to [Hemlock Lake](/wiki/Hemlock_Lake "Hemlock Lake") at the county line. While in Ontario County in the town of [Canadice](/wiki/Canadice,_New_York "Canadice, New York"), NY 15A follows a more inland route to the east, passing through an isolated forest situated between Hemlock and [Canadice Lakes](/wiki/Canadice_Lake "Canadice Lake"). After 6 miles (10 km), the highway reenters Livingston County near the northern end of Hemlock Lake.[[4]](#cite_note-yahoo-4)

[![Shield on the edge of the village of Lima with buildings in the background](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/NY_15A_Northbound_in_Lima.jpg/250px-NY_15A_Northbound_in_Lima.jpg)](/wiki/File:NY_15A_Northbound_in_Lima.jpg)

NY 15A northbound in the village of Lima

Now in the [town of Livonia](/wiki/Livonia_(town),_New_York "Livonia (town), New York"), NY 15A becomes Bald Hill Road as it passes by Hemlock Lake Park, a local park situated at the northeastern tip of the lake. It changes names again just 1.5 miles (2.4 km) later upon entering the hamlet of [Hemlock](/wiki/Hemlock,_New_York "Hemlock, New York"). Here, it becomes Main Street and intersects [US 20A](/wiki/U.S._Route_20A_(New_York) "U.S. Route 20A (New York)"), which forms a [concurrency](/wiki/Concurrency_(road) "Concurrency (road)") with NY 15A along the length of Main Street. The concurrency continues through Hemlock and into rural Livonia, where the 2-mile (3.2 km) overlap ends as US 20A turns west towards [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York"). NY 15A, meanwhile, heads northward into the [town of Lima](/wiki/Lima_(town),_New_York "Lima (town), New York") as Plank Road. It continues across rural terrain for another 3 miles (5 km) to the [village of Lima](/wiki/Lima_(village),_New_York "Lima (village), New York"), where it becomes Lake Avenue. At the village center, NY 15A crosses over [US 20](/wiki/U.S._Route_20_in_New_York "U.S. Route 20 in New York") and [NY 5](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_5 "New York State Route 5") and changes names to Rochester Street. The route leaves the community and its residential surroundings after 0.75 miles (1.21 km), but retains the Rochester Street name until it reaches the Livingston–[Monroe](/wiki/Monroe_County,_New_York "Monroe County, New York") county line west of [Honeoye Falls](/wiki/Honeoye_Falls,_New_York "Honeoye Falls, New York").[[4]](#cite_note-yahoo-4)

### Monroe County

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_15A&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Monroe County")]

Across the county line in the town of [Mendon](/wiki/Mendon,_New_York "Mendon, New York"), NY 15A takes on the name Rush–Lima Road and begins curving to the northwest. It connects to Honeoye Falls twice: first via Main Street at a junction just north of the county line, and later by way of Monroe Street[[4]](#cite_note-yahoo-4) (formerly [NY 363](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_363_(1932%E2%80%931950s) "New York State Route 363 (1932–1950s)")[[5]](#cite_note-5)) at an intersection 1 mile (1.6 km) to the northwest. Past Monroe Street, the route makes a long, significant turn to the west, following [Honeoye Creek](/wiki/Honeoye_Creek "Honeoye Creek") into the town of [Rush](/wiki/Rush,_New_York "Rush, New York") and the hamlet of the same name. The highway meets Rush–West Rush Road at an intersection south of the community, at which point NY 15A turns back to the north and crosses over Honeoye Creek. In the process, NY 15A becomes East Henrietta Road, a name the highway retains until its northern end in [Rochester](/wiki/Rochester,_New_York "Rochester, New York"). On the opposite side of the creek, NY 15A intersects [NY 251](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_251 "New York State Route 251") in the center of Rush.[[4]](#cite_note-yahoo-4)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/NY_15A_north_at_I-390_in_Brighton.jpg/250px-NY_15A_north_at_I-390_in_Brighton.jpg)](/wiki/File:NY_15A_north_at_I-390_in_Brighton.jpg)

NY 15A northbound at I-390 in Brighton. The Erie Canal and the city of Rochester are in the background.

Outside of Rush, NY 15A takes on a more northeasterly alignment as it passes Rush's town reservoir and enters the town of [Henrietta](/wiki/Henrietta,_New_York "Henrietta, New York"). The surroundings initially remain as rural as they were in Rush; however, the amount of development along the highway gradually increases as the route approaches and passes over the [New York State Thruway](/wiki/New_York_State_Thruway "New York State Thruway") ([I-90](/wiki/Interstate_90_in_New_York "Interstate 90 in New York")). One mile (1.6 km) later, NY 15A intersects [NY 253](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_253 "New York State Route 253") in a densely populated area west of [Rush–Henrietta Senior High School](/wiki/Rush%E2%80%93Henrietta_Senior_High_School "Rush–Henrietta Senior High School"). The homes continue for another 0.5 miles (0.8 km) to the Monroe County Fairgrounds (home to [The Dome Center](/wiki/The_Dome_Center "The Dome Center")), at which point NY 15A begins to become lined with commercial establishments. The trend temporarily ceases around an interchange with [I-390](/wiki/Interstate_390 "Interstate 390")—where NY 15A curves northwestward—but resumes ahead of a junction with [NY 252](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_252 "New York State Route 252"), itself a major commercial strip.[[4]](#cite_note-yahoo-4)

North of NY 252, NY 15A passes under the [CSX Transportation](/wiki/CSX_Transportation "CSX Transportation")-owned [West Shore Subdivision](/wiki/West_Shore_Subdivision "West Shore Subdivision") and crosses into [Brighton](/wiki/Brighton,_Monroe_County,_New_York "Brighton, Monroe County, New York"). Just past the town line, the route connects to Clay and Brighton–Henrietta Town Line Roads by way of a grade-separated intersection 0.25 miles (0 km) north of the railroad overpass. It continues onward, passing along the west side of the sprawling [Monroe Community College](/wiki/Monroe_Community_College "Monroe Community College") campus to another interchange with I-390. NY 15A passes over the [Erie Canal](/wiki/Erie_Canal "Erie Canal") and enters the city of Rochester shortly afterward. Once in the city, the route passes by two large office campuses south of Westfall Road and several blocks of homes north of the street before merging back into NY 15 (Mount Hope Avenue) at a junction east of [Strong Memorial Hospital](/wiki/Strong_Memorial_Hospital "Strong Memorial Hospital").[[4]](#cite_note-yahoo-4)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_15A&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/NY_15A_south_in_Livonia.jpg/250px-NY_15A_south_in_Livonia.jpg)](/wiki/File:NY_15A_south_in_Livonia.jpg)

Southbound on NY 15A across rolling terrain in the town of Livonia

What is now [NY 15](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_15 "New York State Route 15") was originally designated as part of [NY 4](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_4_(1924%E2%80%931927) "New York State Route 4 (1924–1927)") when the first set of posted routes in [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") were assigned in 1924.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The route was renumbered to [NY 2](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_2_(1927%E2%80%931939) "New York State Route 2 (1927–1939)") in 1927 to eliminate numerical duplication with the new [US 4](/wiki/U.S._Route_4_in_New_York "U.S. Route 4 in New York") in eastern New York.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Modern NY 15A, meanwhile, was only partially state-maintained by the mid-1920s. In 1926, only two sections of the route were state-owned: from Monroe Street west of [Honeoye Falls](/wiki/Honeoye_Falls,_New_York "Honeoye Falls, New York") north to [Rochester](/wiki/Rochester,_New_York "Rochester, New York"), and the segment of highway in [Livonia](/wiki/Livonia_(town),_New_York "Livonia (town), New York") that modern NY 15A shares with [US 20A](/wiki/U.S._Route_20A_(New_York) "U.S. Route 20A (New York)").[[8]](#cite_note-8) In spite of this fact, all of what is now NY 15A was designated as NY 2A, an alternate route of NY 2 between [Springwater](/wiki/Springwater,_New_York "Springwater, New York") and Rochester, in the [1930 renumbering of state highways in New York](/wiki/1930_state_highway_renumbering_(New_York) "1930 state highway renumbering (New York)").[[2]](#cite_note-gb-2)

The sub-standard sections of the route between Springwater and Hemlock and from north of Hemlock to Honeoye Falls were improved in stages over the next half-decade, beginning with the piece between the villages of [Lima](/wiki/Lima_(village),_New_York "Lima (village), New York") and Honeoye Falls c. 1931.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) The remainder of the Hemlock–Honeoye Falls segment and the entirety of the route south of Hemlock was rebuilt c. 1934.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) NY 2 was replaced in April 1939 by [US 15](/wiki/U.S._Route_15_in_New_York "U.S. Route 15 in New York"), which was extended northward from its previous terminus at the [Pennsylvania state line](/wiki/New_York%E2%80%93Pennsylvania_border "New York–Pennsylvania border") to Rochester. NY 2A was renumbered to NY 15A as a result.[[3]](#cite_note-1939changes-3) NY 15A has not been substantially altered since that time.[[13]](#cite_note-13)
